prompt: Zone pressurization design for stairwells and cleanrooms requires:

options.text: Calculating airflow offsets to maintain required differential pressure under door openings
Only thermostat location in corridors
Ignoring door undercut and leakage paths
Negative pressure in all exit stairs always

explanation: Pressure design is critical in healthcare, labs, and life safety stairs.

distractorExplanations.b: Pressurization design uses airflow and leakage analysis.
distractorExplanations.c: Leakage paths determine required offset airflow.
distractorExplanations.d: Stairwells are typically pressurized positively in fire mode.

prompt: Radiant cooling design must manage:

options.text: Condensation risk on surfaces, dew point control, and integration with ventilation dehumidification
Only sensible loads with no humidity concern
Maximum surface temperatures above space dew point always without sensors
Elimination of all ventilation

explanation: Radiant systems need careful humidity and surface temperature control.

distractorExplanations.b: Radiant panels require dew point control to avoid condensation.
distractorExplanations.c: Controls must prevent condensation on cold surfaces.
distractorExplanations.d: Ventilation still required for IAQ in radiant systems.

prompt: Kitchen makeup air design should:

options.text: Match hood exhaust, condition makeup air, and interlock with hood operation
Provide unlimited unconditioned makeup in all climates
Ignore hood exhaust CFM requirements
Eliminate fire suppression interlocks

explanation: Commercial kitchen design is a specialized high-load application.

distractorExplanations.b: Untreated makeup air imposes large heating/cooling loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Makeup must balance exhaust per code and design.
distractorExplanations.d: Hood systems integrate with fire and HVAC controls.

prompt: Selecting filters for MERV requirements balances:

options.text: Particle removal efficiency, pressure drop, fan energy, and maintenance access
Only filter frame color
Highest MERV with no fan capacity check
No consideration of filter bypass leakage

explanation: IAQ goals must be achievable within fan and coil capacity limits.

distractorExplanations.b: MERV, pressure drop, and energy matter in selection.
distractorExplanations.c: High MERV increases static pressure and fan kW.
distractorExplanations.d: Bypass reduces realized filtration performance.

prompt: Humidification system design in cold climates addresses:

options.text: Water quality, distribution method, condensation on ducts, and energy source selection
Only dehumidification with no winter humidification
Uncontrolled steam injection without pipe slope
Ignoring IAQ impacts of untreated water

explanation: Isothermal vs adiabatic humidification has different energy and hygiene profiles.

distractorExplanations.b: Cold dry climates often need humidification for comfort and materials.
distractorExplanations.c: Improper humidifier installation causes duct damage.
distractorExplanations.d: Water treatment prevents biological and mineral issues.

prompt: Thermal comfort design per ASHRAE 55 considers:

options.text: Operative temperature, humidity, air speed, metabolic rate, and clothing insulation
Only dry-bulb setpoint with no other variables
Structural footing depth exclusively
Only exterior paint reflectance

explanation: Designers apply PMV/PPD or adaptive comfort models as appropriate.

distractorExplanations.b: ASHRAE 55 uses multiple comfort variables.
distractorExplanations.c: Footing design is unrelated to thermal comfort.
distractorExplanations.d: Comfort depends on environmental and personal factors.

prompt: Passive design strategies in HBDP include:

options.text: Orientation, shading, thermal mass, natural ventilation, and high-performance envelope assemblies
Only increasing HVAC capacity without envelope improvements
Eliminating all glazing to remove solar gain always
Ignoring climate-specific design responses

explanation: Integrated design prioritizes load reduction before equipment sizing.

distractorExplanations.b: Passive measures reduce loads before active systems.
distractorExplanations.c: Daylighting and views are balanced with shading design.
distractorExplanations.d: Passive strategies depend on local climate.

prompt: Cooling load peak may differ from heating peak because:

options.text: Solar gains and internal loads drive cooling peaks while heating peaks follow design winter temperature
Heating and cooling always peak at the same hour
Only occupant count affects heating with no envelope loss
Cooling peaks occur only at night without exception

explanation: Designers size equipment for respective peak conditions per method standards.

distractorExplanations.b: Peak conditions differ seasonally and diurnally.
distractorExplanations.c: Envelope transmission dominates heating in many climates.
distractorExplanations.d: Cooling peaks often coincide with afternoon solar and occupancy.

prompt: Refrigerant piping design for split systems must follow:

options.text: Manufacturer limits on line length, lift, oil return, and charge calculations
Only architectural ceiling height with no refrigerant data
Unlimited charge without ventilation per ASHRAE 15/34
Random pipe sizing without velocity guidance

explanation: Safety standards and manufacturer data govern refrigerant system design.

distractorExplanations.b: Piping limits are equipment-specific engineering requirements.
distractorExplanations.c: Refrigerant quantity triggers safety ventilation requirements.
distractorExplanations.d: Oil return and capacity depend on proper pipe sizing.

prompt: Demand control ventilation design modulates outdoor air based on:

options.text: Occupancy or CO₂ concentration while maintaining code minimum ventilation
Fixed maximum outdoor air at all times
Outdoor air zero in all occupied modes
Only static pressure in supply ducts

explanation: DCV saves conditioning energy in variable occupancy spaces.

distractorExplanations.b: DCV reduces OA when occupancy is low.
distractorExplanations.c: Codes require minimum ventilation rates.
distractorExplanations.d: DCV sensors track occupancy or CO₂, not duct static alone.

prompt: High-performance facade design reduces loads by combining:

options.text: Low U-factor assemblies, low SHGC glazing where appropriate, and continuous air barriers
Single-pane clear glass on all orientations always
Discontinuous air barriers for faster construction only
Eliminating all shading devices globally

explanation: Facade performance sets the baseline load for HVAC sizing.

distractorExplanations.b: High-performance facades use improved glazing and assemblies.
distractorExplanations.c: Air barrier continuity is essential for performance.
distractorExplanations.d: Shading reduces unwanted solar gains.

prompt: Pump affinity laws indicate pump power scales approximately with:

options.text: The cube of speed (or flow) ratio for variable-speed applications
Linear relationship with speed only
No relationship between flow and power
Square of building height in stories

explanation: VFDs on variable-load pumps yield significant energy savings.

distractorExplanations.b: Power varies roughly with the cube of speed.
distractorExplanations.c: Hydraulic power increases with flow and head.
distractorExplanations.d: Building height alone does not define pump affinity.

prompt: Laboratory exhaust design must address:

options.text: Fan redundancy, dilution, stack discharge, and makeup air with energy recovery where feasible
Only office carpet selection
Recirculating all lab air to save energy without risk review
No stack height or re-entrainment analysis

explanation: Specialized exhaust is a major energy and safety design focus.

distractorExplanations.b: Labs have high exhaust and containment requirements.
distractorExplanations.c: Labs typically exhaust contaminated air without recirculation.
distractorExplanations.d: Exhaust discharge must avoid intake re-entrainment.

prompt: CHD designers sizing outdoor air per 62.1 VRP use:

options.text: People outdoor air rate plus area outdoor air rate for each zone, then system-level treatment
Only area-based OA with no people component
Zero OA if CO₂ sensors are installed without analysis
Random OA values per designer preference

explanation: System ventilation efficiency may adjust zone-level requirements.

distractorExplanations.b: VRP combines per-person and per-area rates.
distractorExplanations.c: DCV still must meet minimums; IAQ procedure is a separate path.
distractorExplanations.d: 62.1 defines prescribed rate tables.

prompt: Displacement ventilation design delivers:

options.text: Low-velocity supply at floor level relying on stratification and heat plumes
High-velocity ceiling jets mixing room air completely
No relationship between supply temperature and stratification
Only recirculation with no fresh air path

explanation: Displacement ventilation suits high ceilings and suitable cooling loads.

distractorExplanations.b: Displacement uses low-level low-velocity supply.
distractorExplanations.c: Supply temperature must be warmer than occupied zone for displacement.
distractorExplanations.d: Ventilation air must still be introduced appropriately.

prompt: Integrated lighting and HVAC design in HBDP coordinates:

options.text: Heat gain from fixtures, daylighting controls, and plug load impacts on cooling
Only wattage of lobby art lighting with no HVAC impact
Eliminating all daylight sensors globally
No coordination between architects and engineers

explanation: LED retrofits and daylighting change internal gain profiles.

distractorExplanations.b: All internal gains affect cooling loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Daylighting controls reduce lighting heat gain.
distractorExplanations.d: Integrated design requires cross-discipline coordination.

prompt: Sound attenuation in HVAC design uses:

options.text: Duct lining, silencers, and equipment selection to meet NC/RC criteria
Only visual duct layout with no acoustic review
Maximum fan speed regardless of noise
Eliminating all vibration isolation

explanation: Acoustic design is especially critical in theaters, offices, and healthcare.

distractorExplanations.b: Occupant comfort includes noise criteria.
distractorExplanations.c: Design must meet project acoustic requirements.
distractorExplanations.d: Isolation reduces structure-borne noise transmission.

prompt: Geothermal heat exchanger design requires:

options.text: Soil/rock thermal conductivity analysis, borefield sizing, and pump power accounting
Only above-ground air temperature averages
Infinite ground temperature stability without modeling
No integration with heat pump performance curves

explanation: GSHP systems need coupled ground and building load analysis.

distractorExplanations.b: Ground loop design depends on subsurface thermal properties.
distractorExplanations.c: Long-term ground temperature drift must be analyzed.
distractorExplanations.d: Entering water temperature affects heat pump efficiency.

prompt: Smoke management HVAC design modes include:

options.text: Pressurization, exhaust, and purge sequences coordinated with fire alarm inputs
Only normal comfort cooling sequences
No fan restart after power loss
Ignoring door and damper positions

explanation: Life safety sequences are mandatory in many occupancy types.

distractorExplanations.b: Fire/smoke modes override normal operation.
distractorExplanations.c: Emergency power and sequences address smoke control.
distractorExplanations.d: Damper and fan positions are critical smoke control elements.

prompt: CHD/HBDP exam items at the application level may require:

options.text: Reciting unrelated plumbing code section numbers only
Memorizing staff phone directories
Hand calculating seismic base shear exclusively
Selecting appropriate system types and design parameters for a described building scenario

explanation: ASHRAE exams emphasize scenario-based application and analysis.

distractorExplanations.a: Exams test applied HVAC design judgment.
distractorExplanations.b: Technical scenarios drive application-level items.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural seismic calcs are outside CHD/HBDP focus.

prompt: Designing for future flexibility in HBDP may include:

options.text: Oversized vertical shafts, modular plant capacity, and adaptable zoning/control strategies
Permanently fixed airflow with no VAV capability
Eliminating all spare capacity in mechanical rooms
No access panels for maintenance or upgrades

explanation: High-performance design considers operational adaptation over building life.

distractorExplanations.b: Flexibility anticipates changing loads and layouts.
distractorExplanations.c: Future equipment needs space and infrastructure.
distractorExplanations.d: Maintainability supports lifecycle performance.

prompt: Condenser water reset strategies improve efficiency by:

options.text: Raising condenser water temperature at part load to improve chiller lift characteristics where safe
Lowering tower approach to zero physically always
Running chillers at minimum load continuously
Disabling all cooling towers permanently

explanation: Reset controls must respect chiller manufacturer minimum requirements.

distractorExplanations.b: Approach has physical limits based on wet-bulb.
distractorExplanations.c: Reset optimizes variable conditions, not minimum load always.
distractorExplanations.d: Towers reject heat from water-cooled chillers.

prompt: Outdoor design conditions for load calculations are obtained from:

options.text: ASHRAE climatic design data for the project location (dry-bulb, wet-bulb extremes)
Only average monthly conditions with no peak design
Random temperatures chosen by the owner
Interior thermostat setpoints exclusively

explanation: Correct design weather data prevents under- or over-sizing equipment.

distractorExplanations.b: Equipment sizing uses design extremes, not averages only.
distractorExplanations.c: Engineering standards define design conditions.
distractorExplanations.d: Outdoor design weather drives envelope and ventilation loads.

prompt: Hydronic system diversity factors in design:

options.text: Reduce simultaneous peak load assumption when not all zones peak together
Always assume 100% peak on every zone simultaneously without analysis
Eliminate all safety factors globally
Apply only to plumbing fixture units never HVAC

explanation: Proper diversity reduces plant and pipe oversizing while maintaining comfort.

distractorExplanations.b: Diversity acknowledges non-coincident peaks.
distractorExplanations.c: Diversity is engineering judgment, not elimination of margins.
distractorExplanations.d: HVAC hydronic designs use diversity for plant sizing.

prompt: High-performance building water heating design integrates:

options.text: Low-flow fixtures, efficient heat sources, distribution losses, and recirculation control
Only maximum storage temperature without Legionella policy
Uninsulated long recirculation loops always
No hot water demand calculations

explanation: DHW energy is significant in hospitality, healthcare, and multifamily projects.

distractorExplanations.b: Temperature strategy must balance energy and health.
distractorExplanations.c: Distribution losses are major DHW energy use.
distractorExplanations.d: Fixture counts and schedules size DHW systems.
